title: Anomalous Decay of Quantum Resources: The Entanglement Sudden Death Mpemba Effect
authors: Zhilong Liu, Zehua Tian, Jieci Wang
abstract: In classical thermodynamics, the Mpemba effect refers to the counterintuitive observation that hot water can freeze faster than cold water, manifesting as an anomalous crossing of dynamical trajectories. While analogues of this phenomenon have been explored in quantum radiative systems and spin-chain entanglement asymmetry, its connection to the finite-time decoupling of quantum correlations remains elusive. In this Letter, we uncover a distinct quantum Mpemba effect associated with entanglement sudden death (ESD). By analyzing two qubits interacting with local amplitude damping reservoirs, we demonstrate that a more strongly entangled initial state can experience a faster collapse into a separable state than a more weakly entangled one. We provide an exact analytical derivation of the trajectory crossover dynamics and the ESD time. Finally, we map the phase diagram of initial state parameters to delineate the regime where this anomalous entanglement Mpemba effect occurs, offering insights into the control of quantum resource lifetimes in dissipative environments.
summary: This paper discovers a quantum version of the Mpemba effect where higher initial entanglement leads to faster entanglement sudden death. It provides an analytical framework to understand this anomalous decay and proves that the effect's existence is robust even when the dissipation rates of the two qubits are unequal.
main_problem: The paper investigates the 'Entanglement Sudden Death (ESD) Mpemba Effect,' where a more strongly entangled initial state decays to a separable state faster than a more weakly entangled one.
main_result: The authors analytically derive the conditions for trajectory crossing and demonstrate that the boundaries of this anomalous Mpemba region are robust against environmental dissipation asymmetry.
method: The study uses an analytical approach involving the Lindblad master equation, exact derivation of density matrix elements for X-states, and phase diagram mapping.
model_or_system: Two independent two-level subsystems (qubits) interacting with local, zero-temperature bosonic reservoirs via pure amplitude damping (spontaneous emission) described by a Lindblad master equation.
key_observables: Concurrence (as a measure of entanglement), ESD time (tau*ESD), and crossing time (tau_cross).
important_parameters: Decay rates (Gamma_A, Gamma_B), initial state parameters (w(0), d(0)), and the dissipation asymmetry ratio (Gamma_B/Gamma_A).
main_assumptions: Born-Markov and rotating-wave approximations are used; the environment is at zero temperature; the initial states are restricted to a specific class of X-states.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the dissipative model; Fig 2 compares normal decay vs. Mpemba crossing; Fig 3 shows the impact of asymmetric dissipation; Fig 5 plots characteristic timescales and their ratios as functions of the dissipation ratio.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Mpemba effect analogy, defines the two-qubit dissipative model, provides analytical derivations for ESD and crossing times, analyzes the impact of dissipation asymmetry, and concludes with a phase diagram of the effect's boundaries.
why_interesting: It provides new insights into the control of quantum resource lifetimes in dissipative environments and shows how environmental asymmetry affects the dynamics of entanglement decay.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of the dissipative model. A bipar- tite system, consisting of two-level subsystems A and B, is initially prepared in an entangled state and subsequently undergoes local dissi- pation through independent zero-temperature reservoirs. Each system undergoes an amplitude damping process with a decay rate of ΓA and ΓB, respectively. Both systems are coupled to their own independent reservoirs maintained at zero temperature (T = 0).

FIG. 2. Decay of concurrence under symmetric dissipation ΓA = ΓB = 1. (a) Normal concurrence decay for three different initial states: d(0) = 0.4, 0.4, 0.6 and w(0) = 0.35, 0.25, 0.25, respectively. (b) Anomalous concurrence decay for two different initial states: d1(0) = 0.4, w1(0) = 0.25, and d2(0) = 0.8, w2(0) = 0.35. The yellow circle marks the crossing point at (0.29, 0.22) (coordinates rounded to two decimal places).

FIG. 4. Phase diagrams: solid black semicircle is physical boundary (positivity condition). Grey area: no ESD. Green gradient: quantum Mpemba crossing. Yellow: ESD without Mpemba effect. Blue dot marks reference parameters (|w1(0)| = 0.4, d1(0) = 0.25). Panels: (a) symmetric dissipation (ΓA = ΓB = 1), (b) asymmetric dissipation (ΓA = 0.1, ΓB = 10).

FIG. 3. (a) Normal concurrence decay for four different initial states: ΓA=0.1(pink circle), 0.5(blue star), 1(black square), 5(orange triangle), respectively. w(0) = 0.35, d(0) = 0.4. (b) The Mpemba crossing of concurrence Ccross (pink circles) and the corresponding crossing time τcross (blue squares) vary with ΓB. The initial state parameters are fixed as: d1(0) = 0.4, w1(0) = 0.25 and d2(0) = 0.8, w2(0) = 0.35.

FIG. 5. Characteristic timescales and their dimensionless ratios vary as the ratio ΓB/ΓA. The black dashed line (squares) represents the con- currence trajectory crossing time τcross. Orange dot-dashed line (down- ward triangles) and purple dotted line (diamonds) show ESD times τ∗ ESD1 and τ∗ ESD2 for two distinct initial states. Pink solid line (circles) and blue solid line (stars) give the corresponding ratios τcross/τ∗ ESD1 and τcross/τ∗ ESD2. Vertical grey dotted line marks symmetric dissipation (ΓB = ΓA).
